prescribing_info.title: EPINEPHRINE (multiple formulations) Prescribing Information
prescribing_info.content:
EPINEPHRINE - FULL PRESCRIBING INFORMATION

MECHANISM: Non-selective adrenergic agonist (alpha-1, beta-1, beta-2 receptors)

EFFECTS:
- Alpha-1: Vasoconstriction, increased SVR
- Beta-1: Increased heart rate and contractility
- Beta-2: Bronchodilation, vasodilation in skeletal muscle

FORMULATIONS:
- 1:1000 (1 mg/mL): For IM use (anaphylaxis)
- 1:10,000 (0.1 mg/mL): For IV use (cardiac arrest)
- Auto-injectors: EpiPen 0.3 mg, EpiPen Jr 0.15 mg
- Infusion: 1-4 mg in 250-500 mL NS or D5W

ANAPHYLAXIS:

Adults (IM - preferred route):
- 0.3-0.5 mg (0.3-0.5 mL of 1:1000) IM
- Inject in lateral thigh (vastus lateralis)
- May repeat every 5-15 minutes as needed
- Maximum: No absolute maximum in anaphylaxis

Pediatric (IM):
- 0.01 mg/kg (0.01 mL/kg of 1:1000) IM
- Maximum single dose: 0.3 mg if <30 kg, 0.5 mg if ≥30 kg
- May repeat every 5-15 minutes

Auto-Injector Dosing:
- EpiPen (0.3 mg): Weight ≥30 kg
- EpiPen Jr (0.15 mg): Weight 15-30 kg
- <15 kg: Use 1:1000 vial, dose 0.01 mg/kg

IV Epinephrine (Refractory Anaphylaxis/Shock):
- 0.1 mg (1 mL of 1:10,000) slow IV push
- OR Infusion: 1-10 mcg/min, titrate to effect
- Requires cardiac monitoring

CARDIAC ARREST (ACLS):

Adults:
- 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes
- Use 1:10,000 concentration (10 mL)
- May give via endotracheal tube: 2-2.5 mg diluted in 10 mL NS (less reliable)

Pediatric:
- 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 mL/kg of 1:10,000) IV/IO
- Maximum: 1 mg per dose
- Repeat every 3-5 minutes

BRADYCARDIA WITH HYPOTENSION:
- Infusion: 2-10 mcg/min IV
- Titrate to heart rate and blood pressure

SEVERE ASTHMA:
- 0.3-0.5 mg IM (if nebulized beta-agonists ineffective)
- May repeat every 20 minutes x3 doses

VASOPRESSOR INFUSION:

Concentration:
- Typically 4 mg in 250 mL (16 mcg/mL)
- OR 1 mg in 250 mL (4 mcg/mL)

Dosing:
- Start: 1-2 mcg/min
- Titrate: 1-10 mcg/min
- Severe septic shock may require higher doses (up to 20+ mcg/min)

MAXIMUM DOSES:
- Cardiac arrest: No maximum during resuscitation
- Anaphylaxis: No absolute maximum; repeat as needed
- Infusion: Titrate to effect; no absolute maximum

CONTRAINDICATIONS (Relative for life-threatening conditions):
- Cardiac arrest, anaphylaxis: No absolute contraindications
- Caution: CAD, hypertension, hyperthyroidism, angle-closure glaucoma

WARNINGS:
- Arrhythmias: Tachycardia, VF/VT possible
- Hypertension: May cause cerebral hemorrhage
- Pulmonary edema: Possible with high doses
- Tissue necrosis: Avoid subcutaneous injection of 1:10,000
- Extravasation: Causes local necrosis; treat with phentolamine

DRUG INTERACTIONS:
- Beta-blockers: Paradoxical hypertension; may need higher doses
- MAOIs: Exaggerated response; use reduced doses
- Tricyclic antidepressants: Enhanced effect
- Cocaine: Risk of severe hypertension

ADVERSE REACTIONS:
- Palpitations, tachycardia
- Anxiety, tremor
- Headache
- Hypertension
- Nausea/vomiting
- Pallor

PREPARATION OF INFUSION:
- Add 1-4 mg to 250 mL NS or D5W
- 1 mg/250 mL = 4 mcg/mL
- 4 mg/250 mL = 16 mcg/mL
- Protect from light
- Discard if pink or brown discoloration

STORAGE:
- Room temperature or refrigerated
- Protect from light
- Do not use if discolored or contains precipitate
- Auto-injectors: Check expiration date regularly

HOW SUPPLIED:
- 1 mg/mL (1:1000) ampules, vials, prefilled syringes (IM use)
- 0.1 mg/mL (1:10,000) prefilled syringes (IV use)
- 1 mg/10 mL prefilled syringes for cardiac arrest
- EpiPen 0.3 mg, EpiPen Jr 0.15 mg auto-injectors
- Various other auto-injector brands (Auvi-Q, generic)

SOURCE PROVENANCE:
This document summarizes key information from the FDA-approved prescribing information.
FDA Application: Various NDAs/ANDAs
Label Revision: 2024
For complete prescribing information, refer to the official FDA label at: https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/search.cfm?labeltype=all&query=epinephrine+injection

prescribing_info.title: NARCAN (naloxone) Prescribing Information
prescribing_info.content:
NARCAN (NALOXONE HYDROCHLORIDE) - FULL PRESCRIBING INFORMATION

MANUFACTURER: Emergent BioSolutions (nasal spray)

MECHANISM: Opioid antagonist; competitive antagonist at mu, kappa, and delta opioid receptors

INDICATION:
Emergency treatment of known or suspected opioid overdose, as manifested by respiratory and/or central nervous system depression.

FORMULATIONS:
- Nasal spray: 4 mg/0.1 mL (NARCAN Nasal Spray)
- Nasal spray: 8 mg/0.1 mL (KLOXXADO)
- Injection: 0.4 mg/mL vials (generic)
- Injection: 1 mg/mL vials
- Prefilled syringe: 2 mg/2 mL (for IV/IM/SC use)
- Auto-injector: 2 mg/0.4 mL (EVZIO - discontinued but may still be available)

DOSAGE - NASAL SPRAY:

NARCAN Nasal Spray (4 mg):
- Initial: 4 mg (one spray in one nostril)
- If no response or partial response: Additional doses every 2-3 minutes
- Alternate nostrils with each dose
- Maximum: No maximum; repeat as needed until emergency help arrives

KLOXXADO (8 mg):
- Initial: 8 mg (one spray in one nostril)
- If no response: May repeat every 2-3 minutes
- Higher dose for high-potency synthetic opioids (fentanyl)

DOSAGE - INJECTABLE:

IV (Fastest onset):
- Initial: 0.4-2 mg IV
- May repeat every 2-3 minutes until response
- Maximum: No absolute maximum

IM/SC (If no IV access):
- Initial: 0.4-2 mg IM or SC
- May repeat every 2-3 minutes
- IM onset: 2-5 minutes

Opioid-Dependent Patients:
- Start with 0.04-0.1 mg IV
- Titrate slowly to restore respiration without precipitating withdrawal
- Goal: Adequate ventilation, NOT full arousal

Infusion (Sustained overdose from long-acting opioids):
- After initial reversal: 0.4 mg/hour IV
- Titrate to maintain adequate respiration
- May need for several hours (methadone OD can require 24-48 hours)

PEDIATRIC DOSING:
- Initial: 0.1 mg/kg IV/IM/SC
- Maximum initial dose: 2 mg
- May repeat every 2-3 minutes as needed

NEONATAL OPIOID DEPRESSION:
- 0.01 mg/kg IV/IM/SC
- May repeat every 2-3 minutes
- Caution: May precipitate seizures or withdrawal

DURATION OF ACTION:
- Naloxone: 30-90 minutes
- Many opioids last longer than naloxone
- Monitor for re-sedation after initial response
- May need repeated doses or infusion

IMPORTANT CONSIDERATIONS:

Re-narcotization:
- Fentanyl/methadone/buprenorphine may outlast naloxone
- Monitor for at least 2 hours (longer for long-acting opioids)
- May need repeat dosing or infusion

Withdrawal:
- Abrupt reversal in opioid-dependent patients causes:
  - Nausea, vomiting
  - Diaphoresis
  - Tachycardia, hypertension
  - Pulmonary edema (rare)
  - Severe agitation
- Titrate to respiration, not consciousness

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- Known hypersensitivity to naloxone

WARNINGS:
- Abrupt reversal may cause acute opioid withdrawal
- Risk of recurrent respiratory depression (re-narcotization)
- Post-operative use: May reverse analgesia, cause hypertension, VT/VF, pulmonary edema
- Neonates: Risk of seizures

ADVERSE REACTIONS:
- Related to opioid withdrawal:
  - Nausea, vomiting
  - Diaphoresis
  - Tachycardia
  - Tremor
  - Agitation, combativeness
- Rare: Pulmonary edema, arrhythmias

NASAL SPRAY ADMINISTRATION:
- Do NOT prime or test spray
- Insert nozzle into nostril until fingers touch bottom of nose
- Press plunger firmly
- Lay patient on side (recovery position) after giving

STORAGE:
- Nasal spray: Room temperature 15-25°C (59-77°F)
- Protect from light
- Do not freeze
- Check expiration date regularly

AVAILABILITY:
- Nasal spray available over-the-counter (OTC) without prescription
- Injectable forms require prescription in most settings
- Community distribution programs widely available

HOW SUPPLIED:
- NARCAN Nasal Spray: 4 mg/0.1 mL (2-pack or 4-pack)
- KLOXXADO: 8 mg/0.1 mL (2-pack)
- Generic naloxone injection: 0.4 mg/mL, 1 mg/mL vials
- Prefilled syringes: 2 mg/2 mL

SOURCE PROVENANCE:
This document summarizes key information from the FDA-approved prescribing information.
FDA Application: NDA 208411
Label Revision: 03/2023
For complete prescribing information, refer to the official FDA label at: https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/drugInfo.cfm?setid=a9b8c7d6-e5f4-3g2h-1i0j-k9l8m7n6o5p4

prescribing_info.title: N-ACETYLCYSTEINE (NAC) Prescribing Information
prescribing_info.content:
N-ACETYLCYSTEINE (ACETADOTE, MUCOMYST) - PRESCRIBING INFORMATION

BRAND NAMES:
- Acetadote (IV formulation)
- Mucomyst (Oral/Nebulized formulation)

MECHANISM:
- Glutathione precursor
- Replenishes hepatic glutathione stores
- Directly detoxifies NAPQI (toxic acetaminophen metabolite)
- Most effective within 8 hours of ingestion; still beneficial up to 24+ hours

INDICATION:
Antidote for acetaminophen overdose to prevent or lessen hepatic injury.

DOSAGE - IV PROTOCOL (Acetadote):

21-Hour Protocol (FDA-approved):
- Loading: 150 mg/kg IV in 200 mL D5W over 1 hour
- Second infusion: 50 mg/kg IV in 500 mL D5W over 4 hours (12.5 mg/kg/hr)
- Third infusion: 100 mg/kg IV in 1000 mL D5W over 16 hours (6.25 mg/kg/hr)
- Total dose: 300 mg/kg over 21 hours

2-Bag Simplified Protocol (Alternative):
- Loading: 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL over 2 hours
- Maintenance: 200 mg/kg in 1000 mL over 10 hours
- Total: 300 mg/kg over 12 hours
- May continue 200 mg/kg over additional 10 hours if needed

Extended Infusion (For severe poisoning):
- Continue 6.25 mg/kg/hour until:
  - Acetaminophen level undetectable AND
  - ALT declining/normal AND
  - INR <2.0 AND
  - Patient clinically improving

DOSAGE - ORAL PROTOCOL (Mucomyst 20%):

- Loading dose: 140 mg/kg PO
- Maintenance: 70 mg/kg PO every 4 hours x 17 doses
- Total: 1330 mg/kg over 72 hours
- Dilute to 5% solution with juice or soft drink
- If vomiting: Repeat dose within 1 hour or give IV

MAXIMUM DOSES:
- No absolute maximum in acetaminophen overdose
- Continue treatment until liver injury resolving
- Weight-based dosing for all patients

PEDIATRIC DOSING:
- Same mg/kg dosing as adults
- Adjust fluid volumes:
  - <20 kg: Use decreased diluent volumes per package insert
  - Avoid fluid overload in small children

WHEN TO TREAT:
- Acetaminophen level above treatment line on Rumack-Matthew nomogram
- Single acute ingestion >150 mg/kg or >7.5 g total
- Unknown time of ingestion with detectable APAP and elevated AST/ALT
- Repeated supratherapeutic ingestion with elevated ALT

ANAPHYLACTOID REACTIONS (IV NAC):

Symptoms (typically with loading dose):
- Flushing, pruritus, rash
- Nausea, vomiting
- Bronchospasm
- Hypotension (rare)
- Angioedema (rare)

Management:
- Most reactions mild; slow or temporarily stop infusion
- Give antihistamines (diphenhydramine 50 mg IV)
- Bronchospasm: Albuterol
- Severe reactions: Stop infusion, treat as anaphylaxis, may restart at slower rate
- True anaphylaxis is extremely rare

CONTRAINDICATIONS:
- Known hypersensitivity (rare)
- Relative: Asthma (nebulized form may trigger bronchospasm)

WARNINGS:
- Anaphylactoid reactions (IV administration)
- Fluid overload risk with IV protocol
- GI upset with oral administration
- Sulfur odor may cause nausea

DRUG INTERACTIONS:
- Activated charcoal: May reduce NAC absorption (give NAC 1-2 hours after charcoal if both needed; IV NAC preferred)
- Nitrates: Enhanced hypotensive effect

ADVERSE REACTIONS:
- Nausea, vomiting (especially oral)
- Anaphylactoid reactions (IV): Rash, pruritus, bronchospasm
- Stomatitis (nebulized use)

MONITORING:
- Acetaminophen levels (until undetectable)
- AST, ALT, INR (until improving)
- Signs/symptoms of hepatic failure
- Anaphylactoid reactions during IV infusion

FORMULATIONS:
- Acetadote: 20% (200 mg/mL) IV solution
- Mucomyst: 20% (200 mg/mL) solution for oral/nebulized use
- Mucomyst: 10% (100 mg/mL) solution

STORAGE:
- Unopened vials: Room temperature
- After opening: Refrigerate, use within 96 hours
- Discard if color changes to purple

HOW SUPPLIED:
- Acetadote: 20% 30 mL single-dose vials (6 g per vial)
- Mucomyst: 20% 4 mL, 10 mL, 30 mL vials

SOURCE PROVENANCE:
This document summarizes key information from the FDA-approved prescribing information.
FDA Application: NDA 018888/Various
Label Revision: 2023
For complete prescribing information, refer to the official FDA label at: https://dailymed.nlm.nih.gov/dailymed/search.cfm?labeltype=all&query=acetylcysteine
